content:
## Sequence Transduction Models

In deep learning, **Sequence Transduction Models** represent the broad family of neural network architectures dedicated to mapping an arbitrary-length input sequence to an arbitrary-length output sequence. Because the output sequence length may differ from the input length, these models cannot rely on simple point-to-point regression or classification. Instead, they form the foundational architecture for complex tasks like speech recognition, text summarization, and machine translation. The overarching research goal in this domain is to develop highly efficient models that can accurately transduce these sequences while overcoming the sequential processing bottlenecks of older architectures [[object:397eed09-adea-5051-9457-0f511cb7c62e]]. 

Historically, Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)—including advanced variants like Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)—have served as the standard for sequence transduction. While highly capable, these legacy architectures inherently constrain how information flows through the network. They typically process data sequentially, which limits parallelization during training and makes it computationally difficult to capture long-range dependencies within the sequence. To push the boundaries of what these models can achieve, newer architectures like the Transformer have been introduced to improve upon these exact foundational sequence transduction methods, relying entirely on attention mechanisms rather than recurrence [[object:da0119a8-7810-5f01-8835-ec41b296904d]].

### Try it
You are tasked with building a model to automatically convert raw audio recordings of spoken English into written English text. 
1. Identify whether this task requires a sequence transduction model.
2. Briefly explain how the relationship between the input and output sequences justifies this architectural choice.
